paragraphs.text: virtue of having the closest contact with the body, has become intertwined with it, therefore all the needs of the body are to be considered as its needs, too. That is why we say: “I want to eat, drink, sleep. I want to walk, stroll around, work. I want to see various colors, hear various things, smell various smells,” and the like. Having assimilated to itself all the body’s needs, the soul considers the satisfaction of these needs to be its business, and thus concerns itselfwith food, drink, sleep, clothing, shelter and other like things, desiring in every way to make the body comfortable so that it will not bother it with its annoying needs.

This relationship of the soul to the body, which is a one-sided relationship, comes from a certain inner compulsion, which is found in it as a sort of instinct, creature-love, love for the body, the desire to tyiaintain the cotnjort of the body and obtain all that is needed for it.

The sum total of all this comprises the physical aspect of human life. However, not everything here is equally physical, or carnal and sensual. Only the digestive system is strongly carnal; but even it is ennobled by the adaptation of its satisfaction to strictly mental needs and purposes. The organs of movement and feeling serve mental needs more than bodily ones. One organ, which stands apart from the system of other organs, the organ of speech, is an organ exclusively of the soul, which alone it is intended to serve. ^X^at is undesirable about the physical, carnal and sensual life in the moral sense is when a person who is extremely attracted to things of the belly and the body sets as his main goal the concern for bodily comfort, or the complete gratification of physical needs only, forgetting about mental needs, and even more about spiritual needs. In this case, each physical need, simple in nature, branches out into a diversity of needs inculcated by habit, and an inclination for various means of gratification. Take food, drink or clothing, for example. What could 47
THE SPIRITUAL LIFE be more basic than these things! Meanwhile, how many needs are constant: Give it to me if it kills me!

With this in mind, we see that we are not always free, because we are always chasing after needs to satisfy them; at the same time, dozens of other people are doing exactly the same thing. The minds and spirits of these people are inevitably famished, if not completely deadened, stopped up, and immersed in sensuality.
Think over all this, if you will. As for the mind, that will be for the next letter.

THE LIFE OF THE SOUL THE INTELLECTUAL ASPECT The second realm ofhuman life: the life ofthe soul and her three primary categories. Thefirst category: the intellectual aspect with its operations. Knowledge and science. The normal course of thought and the empty wandering ofthe thoughts.

You have previously gone inward; let us enter within once again. See what a multiplicity and variety of actions and motions there are! First one, then another enters and exits; takes root, then is rejected; does something, and does it again. This is because the soul is in continual motion, and is unable to stand still on one thing. If we were to begin to look at the soul as a whole, we would not be able to make out anything, and so we must divide it into parts, and then examine each part individually. We have already long since looked over and divided all motions of the soul into three categories: thoughts, desires and senses, each of which has received its name from a particular aspect of the soul—the intellectual (thinking), the desiring, and
